stats.headline: Tied for the **#1 most-tested PSLE Science topic** — **11.4% of total PSLE marks** (2020–2024). More than half are open-ended, and the high-mark OEQs almost ALL follow the **same 4-step electromagnet recipe**.

keyConcepts.title: Electrical Circuits: How important is this?
keyConcepts.body: Tied with "Interaction of Forces" for the most-tested PSLE Science topic. The MCQs test diagram reading; the OEQs test whether you can WRITE the explanation step by step.
bullets: **18 PSLE questions** in 2020–2024 (8 MCQ, 10 OEQ)
**42 marks** total across those 5 papers — about one in nine PSLE Science marks
**~56% of the questions are OEQ** — and the OEQs include the 4 and 5-mark monsters

**We will cover the 4 concept families and 3 worked examples:**
  • Reading the circuit diagram
  • Series vs Parallel — the fuse rule
  • Bulb brightness — same loop, same batteries?
  • Electromagnets — the 4-step OEQ formula
keyConcepts.callout: If you only revise one Physical Science topic — start here. The electromagnet OEQ alone is worth ~4-5 marks every year.

keyConcepts.title: 1: Reading the circuit diagram
keyConcepts.body: Before you answer anything, READ the diagram. PSLE circuit diagrams look complicated because of the layout, but they're built from 4 ingredients: **battery, bulb(s), switch, wire**. Trace the wire with your pencil to find the loops.
bullets: **Bulb** ⊗ — lights up only when current flows through it. See above diagram on how bulb needs to be connected for current to flow.

**Battery** ▎▏ — the longer line is **+**, the shorter line is **−**. Two batteries in a row = **series** (more voltage); side-by-side facing the same way = **parallel** (same voltage but lasts longer).
**Switch** — open = no current; closed = current flows.
**Wire** — just a path. Follow it from + terminal of battery, through every component, back to − terminal.
**The 'loop' question** — does current have a path from + back to −? **Yes → closed circuit, bulb lights up.** **No → open circuit, bulb stays dark.**
**Common trap** — wires that LOOK separate may actually be joined at a junction (dot). Wires that LOOK joined may actually cross without touching (no dot). The dot is what matters.

**The bulb-wiring trap** (see diagram above) — a bulb only lights when wires touch BOTH metal contacts:
   • One wire on the **BOTTOM TIP** (the small dot at the base — the "foot contact").
   • The OTHER wire on the **SIDE OF THE SCREW THREAD** (the ribbed metal barrel).
If both wires touch the SAME contact (both on the screw thread, or both on the tip), or if one touches the **glass**, the circuit is incomplete and the bulb stays dark. PSLE MCQs love this trap: "When the switch was closed, why didn't the bulb light up?" — check where the wires actually make contact.
keyConcepts.callout: First move on every circuit question: **trace the loop with your pencil**. If you can't get from + back to − without lifting your pencil, it's an open circuit.

keyConcepts.title: 2: Series vs Parallel — the fuse rule
keyConcepts.body: Most PSLE circuit MCQs hinge on the difference between series and parallel. There's one shortcut that handles almost every "which bulb blew" or "bulb fuses, what happens" question.
bullets: **Series circuit** — one path. Current goes through ALL bulbs in order. **If ANY bulb fuses → ALL bulbs go dark.** (Christmas lights, classic example.)
**Parallel circuit** — multiple paths. Each bulb sits on its own branch. **If one bulb fuses → only THAT bulb goes dark; the others stay lit.**

**The shortcut** — when a question says 'one bulb was blown and the others did not light up', the blown bulb is on a SERIES path that ALL the others depend on. It's usually the bulb closest to the battery on a single shared wire.
**Combined circuits** — most PSLE diagrams mix series and parallel. Treat each branch separately: a bulb in series WITHIN a parallel branch only kills its own branch when it fuses.

keyConcepts.title: 2b: Worked example — Which bulb was blown? (PSLE 2020 Q17, 2m)
keyConcepts.body: **Study the circuit. When one bulb was blown, the OTHER bulbs did not light up. Which bulb was blown?**
bullets:
**Step 1 — Find the bulb whose failure breaks the path for ALL the others.** Trace each candidate.
   • Bulb A is on the top-right branch — a parallel arm. Removing it kills only that branch; others stay lit.
   • Bulb C is on the far-left branch — a parallel arm. Removing it kills only its own branch.
   • Bulbs B and D are on inner branches; removing either kills only its own loop.

**Step 2 — Spot the bulb on the COMMON wire.** Look for the bulb sitting on a wire that EVERY other branch's current has to flow through. That's the bulb at the bottom — it's in series with the battery, on the only wire that returns to the negative terminal.
**Step 3 — Verify.** If that bottom bulb fuses, ALL the other branches lose their return path → no current anywhere → every other bulb goes dark. ✓ matches the question.
**Step 4 — Answer.** The bulb on the **main return wire** (the bottom shared bulb) is the one that was blown.
keyConcepts.callout: **Series = group dies together. Parallel = each bulb stands alone.** In a mixed series-parallel diagram, the bulb whose failure kills EVERYTHING is the one on the SHARED wire — the bottleneck every parallel branch depends on.

keyConcepts.title: 3: Bulb brightness — when do two circuits match?
keyConcepts.body: A common MCQ type: 4 circuits drawn, "in which circuit is bulb L brightest?" or "which circuit has the same brightness as the one shown?" Solving by intuition fails. Use the counting method.
bullets: **Step 1 — Count the batteries in series along ONE path** from + back to −. More batteries in series → more voltage → brighter.

**Step 2 — Count how many bulbs share that voltage on the same loop.** If two bulbs are in series, each gets HALF the voltage → dimmer. If one bulb sits alone on its branch, it gets the full voltage → brightest.
**Step 3 — Two circuits have the SAME brightness when they have the same RATIO of batteries-in-series to bulbs-in-series on the loop containing the bulb you care about.**

**Quick test** — 2 batteries + 1 bulb in series gives the same brightness as 1 battery + half a bulb (impossible — so brighter). 1 battery + 2 bulbs in series gives the same brightness as half a battery + 1 bulb (dimmer).
**Common trap** — adding MORE batteries in PARALLEL does NOT make the bulb brighter. Same voltage, just lasts longer.
keyConcepts.callout: **More batteries in series = brighter. More bulbs in series = dimmer.** Adding things in parallel doesn't change brightness — only makes the circuit last longer (extra battery) or adds independent branches (extra bulb).

keyConcepts.title: 4: Electromagnets — the 4-step OEQ formula
keyConcepts.body: 5 of 7 PSLE OEQs on this topic involve electromagnets — bell ringers, buzzers, door locks, door bolts. They all describe a device with a **coil of wire around an iron core**, and ask "explain what happens when the switch is closed". The marking scheme rewards naming each step in a fixed chain.
bullets: **Step 1 — Closed circuit.** 'When the switch is closed, a **closed circuit** is formed and **electric current flows** through the wire coil.'
**Step 2 — Coil becomes an electromagnet.** 'The fixed metal cylinder (with the coil around it) **becomes an electromagnet**.'
**Step 3 — Magnetism attracts.** 'The electromagnet **attracts** the iron [bolt / bar / rod / hammer], causing it to **move** [direction].'
**Step 4 — Physical outcome.** Connect the motion to the device's job (hammer hits the bell, bolt moves away from the catch, bar swings towards point P, etc.).

**Step 5 — What happens when the switch OPENS?** Some questions add this. 'When the switch is opened, the circuit is broken → no current → the cylinder **loses its magnetism / demagnetises** → no longer attracts → iron [bolt / bar] returns to its original position (pulled by the spring).'

scoringExample.scenario: Peter built a door lock using a switch, battery, and a coil of wire around a metal cylinder. When the switch closes, the iron bolt moves away from the catch and the door unlocks. Explain why.

oneMark.label: 1 mark (incomplete)
oneMark.text: The iron bolt moves because of magnetism.

fullMarks.label: Full marks (the 4-step recipe)
fullMarks.text: When the switch is closed, a **closed circuit** is formed, **electricity flows** through the circuit and the fixed metal cylinder **becomes an electromagnet**. The electromagnet **attracts** the iron bolt, pulling it to the right and **away from the catch**, so the door unlocks.

keyConcepts.callout: **Closed circuit → current flows → cylinder becomes electromagnet → attracts iron → motion.** Memorise that chain. It's the same answer for every electromagnet OEQ — only the object names change.

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 1 — Bulb brightness (PSLE 2020 Q21, 2m)
keyConcepts.body: **Identical bulbs R, S and T are used in the circuit. Which change will allow all bulbs to light up with equal brightness?**
bullets: **Step 1 — Trace the current path.** The battery powers the loop. Bulb S sits on the TOP branch (alone). Bulbs R and T sit on the BOTTOM branch (in series with each other).

**Step 2 — Compare voltages per bulb.**
   • S has the full battery voltage to itself → **bright**.
   • R and T share the same voltage → each gets half → **dim**.

**Step 3 — What change equalises them?** All three bulbs need the SAME share of the battery.
   • Connect R and T in **parallel** (same as S) instead of in series with each other.
   • Then S, R, and T are all on their own branch, each gets the full voltage → all bright equally.
**Step 4 — Answer.**
**→ Option (2): Add a wire so that R and T are in parallel. ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Series shares voltage; parallel splits the current.** When bulbs need to match in brightness, give each one its own branch back to the battery.

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 2 — Electromagnet door lock (PSLE 2020 Q39, 5m)
keyConcepts.body: **Peter built a door lock as shown. When he closed the switch, the iron bolt moved to the right, away from the catch and the door was unlocked. (b) Explain how the door unlocks when the switch is closed. [4m]**
bullets: **Step 1 — Closed circuit.** When the switch is closed, a **closed circuit** is formed and **electric current flows** through the wire coiled around the metal cylinder.
**Step 2 — Cylinder becomes an electromagnet.** The current flowing through the coil turns the **fixed metal cylinder** into an **electromagnet**.
**Step 3 — Magnetism attracts the iron bolt.** The electromagnet **attracts the iron bolt** (because iron is a magnetic material). The bolt moves to the **right**, towards the cylinder.
**Step 4 — Physical outcome.** The iron bolt moves **away from the catch** on the door, so the catch is no longer held and the **door unlocks**.

**Bonus (Step 5) if asked about opening the switch:** when the switch opens, current stops → cylinder loses magnetism → bolt no longer attracted → **spring pushes it back** to the left, into the catch → door locks again.
keyConcepts.callout: **Closed circuit → current flows → cylinder becomes electromagnet → attracts iron bolt → bolt moves away from catch.** Same 4-step recipe every electromagnet question. Memorise once, write it out exactly on the exam.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put it all together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style questions on circuits — diagram reading, series/parallel, bulb brightness, and the electromagnet OEQ.
bullets: **6 MCQ** + **4 OEQ** — a cross-section across all 4 sub-topics.
After you submit, the AI flags the trick families you got wrong and suggests a focused follow-up quiz.
Mistakes are saved — we'll return to them in an auto-revision session in **1 week**.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: A complete loop from the + of the battery, through every wire and component, back to the −. Current flows; bulbs light up.
A broken loop — somewhere the wire doesn't reach back to the battery. No current flows. Bulbs stay dark.
Components on the same single wire. Current goes through ALL of them in order. If one bulb fuses, all go dark.
Components on separate branches that join up at junctions. Each branch is its own loop. If one bulb fuses, the others stay lit.
A coil of wire around an iron core that becomes magnetic ONLY when current flows through the coil.
A break in the wire you can open or close. Open = no current. Closed = current flows.
How hard the battery pushes the current. More batteries in series = more voltage = brighter bulb.
A bulb has 'fused' or 'blown' when its filament has snapped — it's now an open wire and won't light up.

subTopics.label: Series vs Parallel
subTopics.description: Series vs parallel arrangement and the fuse rule. 'Which bulb was blown?' / 'When the switch closed, only N bulbs lit up' / how many bulbs go off when one fuses. Tests reading shared vs branched current paths.

subTopics.label: Bulb brightness
subTopics.description: Comparing brightness across multiple circuits. 'In which circuit is L brightest?' / 'In which circuit will L = M?' Solved by counting batteries-in-series and bulbs-in-series along the loop with the named bulb.

subTopics.label: Electromagnets
subTopics.description: Electromagnet OEQs — bell ringer, buzzer, door lock, door bolt. Marking rewards naming each step: closed circuit → current → coil becomes electromagnet → attracts iron → physical motion. 4-5 marks each.

subTopics.label: General circuits
subTopics.description: Catch-all for circuit questions that don't fit the three sharper buckets — diagram reading basics, identifying components, open vs closed circuit, junction traps, and the bulb-contact wiring trap.
